definition: The net effect of demographics on inflation is genuinely ambiguous: the empirical base rate across Japan, Germany, and Korea favors disinflation, but the Goodhart-Pradhan labor scarcity thesis argues for inflation. The uncertainty itself may be tradeable via inflation risk premium.
evidence: Historical base rate: Japan, Germany, Korea all experienced disinflation during demographic decline (3-for-3)
Goodhart-Pradhan thesis: labor scarcity → wage pressure → higher equilibrium inflation
The prior disinflationary era may have been a one-time structural shift (China/Eastern Europe integration)
Uncertainty is a source of inflation risk premium in breakevens
open_questions: Whether services-dominant economies transmit demographic shocks differently than manufacturing-heavy analogues
Whether the post-integration era will produce different inflation outcomes
